questions.question: You have a task routing system where task descriptions are free-form and unpredictable. New task types emerge monthly. Which routing strategy is MOST appropriate as the primary strategy?
options: Deterministic routing rules — most reliable and auditable
LLM-based routing — handles free-form descriptions and novel task types
Round-robin routing — fairest distribution across agents
Manual routing — ensures human oversight for all tasks
questions.explanation: LLM-based routing handles free-form descriptions and novel task types that can't be captured by deterministic rules. Deterministic rules require upfront knowledge of all task types, which this scenario lacks.

questions.question: Agent A hands off to Agent B with only the result string: 'Analysis complete. Revenue is down 12%.' Agent B needs to produce recommendations. What is the PRIMARY problem with this handoff?
options: The result is too long
Context loss — Agent B doesn't know the original goal, data sources, or what period the 12% refers to
The handoff uses the wrong protocol
Agent B should have been run concurrently with Agent A
questions.explanation: This is the thin handoff anti-pattern. Agent B receives the conclusion but not the context needed to use it correctly: what time period? which revenue stream? what data sources? what caveats apply? Without this context, Agent B's recommendations may be incorrect or inappropriate.

questions.question: You're running 50 concurrent research agents all calling the same external search API. The API allows 10 requests per second. What mechanism should you use to prevent rate limit errors?
options: Circuit breaker — it will automatically stop calls when rate limits are hit
Dead-letter queue — failed calls will be retried later
Semaphore-based concurrency control limiting concurrent API calls
Load balancing — distribute calls across multiple API endpoints
questions.explanation: A semaphore limits the number of concurrent API calls, preventing rate limit errors by ensuring you never exceed the API's rate limit. A circuit breaker helps recover after rate limits are hit, but doesn't prevent hitting them in the first place.

questions.question: Two agents read shared state version 5 simultaneously, both make updates, and both write. The final state only contains one agent's changes. What is this failure called and how is it prevented?
options: Deadlock — prevented by using timeouts
Race condition / lost update — prevented by using compare-and-swap with version checking
Context bleed — prevented by namespacing state
Silent failure — prevented by schema validation
questions.explanation: This is the lost update problem, a classic race condition in concurrent state management. Compare-and-swap with version checking prevents it: each write must specify the expected version, and writes that encounter a version mismatch fail and must retry.

questions.question: A circuit breaker has been triggered OPEN after 5 consecutive failures. 75 seconds later (configured timeout is 60s), a new request comes in. What happens?
options: The request fails immediately — the circuit stays open until manually reset
The circuit transitions to HALF-OPEN and allows one trial request through
The circuit automatically transitions to CLOSED and allows all requests
The request is queued until the circuit closes
questions.explanation: After the open timeout expires, the circuit transitions to HALF-OPEN, allowing one trial request. If the trial succeeds, the circuit transitions back to CLOSED. If it fails, it returns to OPEN with a reset timeout.

questions.question: Which merge strategy is MOST appropriate when 5 parallel agents each produce an independent assessment of whether a transaction is fraudulent (yes/no)?
options: Concatenation — combine all assessments into a list
LLM synthesis — have a synthesizer agent read all assessments and write a summary
Voting — the majority decision wins, with confidence = votes/total
Priority ordering — use the most specialized agent's assessment only
questions.explanation: Voting is ideal for decisions where multiple independent agents make the same yes/no judgment. The majority wins, and confidence (votes/total) tells you how strong the consensus is. A split vote can trigger human review.

questions.question: An agent retries a 'send_welcome_email' task after a timeout. The user receives two welcome emails. What principle was violated?
options: Circuit breaker isolation
Idempotency — the operation was retried but was not idempotent
Dead-letter queuing
Concurrency control
questions.explanation: Idempotency was violated. The 'send_welcome_email' operation is not idempotent — executing it twice has different effects than executing it once. Only idempotent operations should be automatically retried. Non-idempotent operations require deduplication mechanisms or manual retry confirmation.

questions.question: In a fan-out/fan-in system with 10 workers, 3 fail with timeout errors and 7 succeed. The task requires at least 8 successful results. What is the appropriate handling strategy?
options: Proceed with the 7 results — partial is better than none
Fail immediately — any partial failure means the whole task fails
Retry the 3 failed tasks; if they still fail after retry, escalate or use DLQ
Run all 10 tasks again from the start
questions.explanation: The timeout errors are likely transient. Retrying the 3 failed tasks is the appropriate first step. If they succeed, you'll meet the 8-result minimum. If they fail again after retry, escalate or dead-letter those tasks. Running all 10 from scratch wastes the 7 successful results.
